questions.question:
Consider the following statements.
I. Symbol table is accessed only during lexical analysis and syntax analysis.
II. Compilers for programming languages that support recursion necessarily need heap storage for memory allocation in the run-time environment.
III. Errors violating the condition 'any variable must be declared before its use' are detected during syntax analysis.

Which of the above statements is/are TRUE?
A. I only
B. II and III only
C. II only
D. None of I, II and III
questions.answer: D. None of I, II and III
questions.to_find: Which of the three statements about compiler phases are TRUE

understand.plain: This question checks knowledge about three common misconceptions in compiler design: how widely the symbol table is used, what storage model recursion requires, and which phase checks declaration-before-use. Each statement contains a subtle error, making all three FALSE.

keywords.explain: A data structure maintained by the compiler that stores identifiers, their types, scopes, and other attributes. It is accessed across multiple phases, not just lexical and syntax analysis.
keywords.example: Semantic analysis looks up symbol table entries to check type compatibility; code generation uses it for variable addresses.

keywords.explain: Dynamically allocated memory managed at runtime. Recursion requires a runtime stack (activation records), not heap storage.
keywords.example: A recursive function pushes a new stack frame for each call; heap is for dynamic allocation like malloc/new.

keywords.explain: The compiler phase that checks meaning and context of the program, including type checking and declaration-before-use rules.
keywords.example: Using an undeclared variable is a semantic error caught in semantic analysis, not syntax analysis.

understand.visual_alt: Three compiler phases: Lexical Analysis -> Syntax Analysis -> Semantic Analysis -> Intermediate Code Generation -> Code Optimization -> Code Generation. Symbol table is consulted by all phases from lexical analysis through code generation.

given.aim: Determine which of the three statements about compiler internals are correct

terms.meaning: Claims symbol table is accessed only during lexical and syntax analysis
terms.example: Symbol table is also used in semantic analysis, intermediate code generation, and code generation
terms.connects: FALSE - symbol table is used by virtually all compiler phases

terms.meaning: Claims recursion requires heap storage for run-time memory allocation
terms.example: Recursive calls use a call stack (LIFO stack of activation records), not heap
terms.connects: FALSE - recursion needs a runtime stack, not heap; heap is for dynamic/manual allocation

terms.meaning: Claims declaration-before-use errors are detected in syntax analysis
terms.example: Syntax analysis only checks grammar structure; undeclared variable is a semantic constraint
terms.connects: FALSE - this is a semantic check, caught in semantic analysis phase

given.plan: Evaluate each statement independently against known compiler theory. Statement I is false because symbol table is used across all phases. Statement II is false because recursion uses stack, not heap. Statement III is false because declaration-before-use is a semantic rule, not a syntactic one.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I: Symbol table usage
steps.apply: The symbol table is first populated during lexical/syntax analysis, but it is read and updated in semantic analysis (type checking, scope resolution), intermediate code generation (variable offsets), and code generation (register allocation, addressing). So it is NOT accessed only during lexical and syntax analysis. Statement I is FALSE.
steps.note: Symbol table is a global shared resource across nearly all compiler phases.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II: Recursion and storage
steps.apply: Recursion requires a call stack to store activation records (local variables, return address, parameters) for each recursive call. Heap is used for dynamic memory allocation (malloc, new). A recursive program does NOT necessarily need heap storage. Statement II is FALSE.
steps.note: Stack is LIFO; each function call pushes an activation record, recursion is perfectly handled by the stack alone.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement III: Declaration-before-use errors
steps.apply: Syntax analysis (parsing) checks only whether the token sequence conforms to the grammar rules (e.g., correct statement structure). The constraint 'a variable must be declared before use' requires checking the symbol table to see if the identifier was previously declared — this is a semantic constraint enforced in semantic analysis. Statement III is FALSE.
steps.note: A grammar rule cannot capture whether a name was previously declared; that requires context, which is semantic.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: All three statements I, II, and III are FALSE. Therefore the correct answer is D. None of I, II and III.
steps.note: This question tests precise understanding of phase boundaries and responsibilities in a compiler.

solution.result: D. None of I, II and III — all three statements are incorrect.

questions.formula_note: Compiler phase responsibilities: symbol table is global to all phases; recursion uses stack (not heap); declaration-before-use is a semantic rule.

questions.to_find: Which compiler phase will detect the error caused by using 'Integer' as a type in C

understand.plain: The program uses 'Integer' as a type name, but in C (ANSI C), there is no built-in type called 'Integer' (it is valid in Java, not C). The question asks which compiler phase catches this mistake. Since 'Integer' is a valid identifier token and the statement structure looks syntactically correct, it passes lexical and syntax analysis but fails semantic analysis where the meaning (type lookup) is checked.

keywords.explain: Breaks source code into tokens (identifiers, keywords, operators, literals). It does not know whether an identifier is a valid type name.
keywords.example: 'Integer' is tokenized as an identifier — perfectly legal as a token.

keywords.explain: Checks meaning: type compatibility, declaration before use, valid type names, etc. It will find that 'Integer' is not a declared type in C.
keywords.example: Using an undeclared type name like 'Integer' in C is caught here when the compiler looks it up in the symbol table and does not find a typedef or built-in.

understand.visual_alt: Source code flows through: Lexical Analyzer (tokenizes 'Integer' as identifier, OK) -> Syntax Analyzer (statement structure is valid, OK) -> Semantic Analyzer (looks up 'Integer' as type, not found, ERROR).

given.aim: Identify the phase that detects an undeclared/invalid type name 'Integer' in ANSI C

terms.meaning: Lexically, 'Integer' is a valid identifier token (starts with a letter, no special chars)
terms.connects: Lexical phase passes this without error

terms.meaning: The structure 'TypeName VarName;' is grammatically valid C syntax
terms.example: 'Integer x;' matches the declaration grammar rule, so parser does not complain
terms.connects: Syntax analysis passes this without error

terms.meaning: Semantic analysis verifies that 'Integer' is a known type (built-in or typedef'd). In C, only int, char, float, double, etc. are built-in; 'Integer' is not defined anywhere.
terms.example: Symbol table lookup for 'Integer' as a type fails, causing a semantic error

given.plan: Trace the program through each phase. Lexical analysis only tokenizes — 'Integer' is a valid identifier. Syntax analysis checks grammar — 'Integer x;' is structurally valid. Semantic analysis checks that 'Integer' is a valid type in scope — it is not, so it throws an error.

steps.title: Lexical Analysis
steps.apply: The lexer scans the program and produces tokens: keyword 'int', identifier 'main', punctuation '(', ')', '{', identifier 'Integer', identifier 'x', ';', keyword 'return', integer '0', ';', '}'. 'Integer' is a perfectly valid identifier token. No lexical error.
steps.note: Lexer only checks token-level structure (valid characters, no unrecognized symbols).

steps.title: Syntax Analysis
steps.apply: The parser checks the token stream against C grammar rules. The declaration 'Integer x;' matches the grammar rule: declaration -> type-specifier declarator ';'. At parse time, any identifier can act as a type specifier if it appears in that syntactic position. The overall structure is syntactically valid. No syntax error.
steps.note: In C, type names and variable names are syntactically indistinguishable without semantic context.

steps.title: Semantic Analysis
steps.apply: Semantic analysis looks up 'Integer' in the symbol table to verify it is a declared type (built-in or typedef). 'Integer' is not a built-in C type and is not typedef'd anywhere in the program. The semantic analyzer reports: 'Integer' is an undeclared identifier / unknown type name. ERROR is thrown here.
steps.note: This is a classic semantic error — undeclared type. In Java, 'Integer' is a valid class, but C has no such built-in.

solution.result: C. Semantic analyzer — it detects that 'Integer' is not a valid type name in ANSI C.

questions.formula_note: Semantic analysis validates type names against the symbol table; undeclared identifiers used as types are semantic errors, not lexical or syntactic ones.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the front-end and back-end of a compiler.
S1: The front-end includes phases that are independent of the target hardware.
S2: The back-end includes phases that are specific to the target hardware.
S3: The back-end includes phases that are specific to the programming language used in the source code.

Identify the CORRECT option:
A. Only S1 is TRUE
B. Only S1 and S2 are TRUE
C. S1, S2 and S3 are all TRUE
D. Only S1 and S3 are TRUE
questions.answer: B. Only S1 and S2 are TRUE
questions.to_find: Which statements about compiler front-end and back-end are TRUE

understand.plain: A compiler is traditionally divided into a front-end (language-dependent, machine-independent phases) and a back-end (language-independent, machine-dependent phases). S1 says front-end is independent of target hardware (correct). S2 says back-end is specific to target hardware (correct). S3 says back-end is specific to the programming language (wrong — the back-end works on intermediate representation, so it is language-independent).

keywords.explain: The phases that analyze the source language: lexical analysis, syntax analysis, semantic analysis, and intermediate code generation. These are language-specific and machine-independent.
keywords.example: A C front-end produces IR; a Java front-end also produces IR. The back-end can then target any machine from the same IR.

keywords.explain: The phases that translate intermediate representation to target machine code: code optimization (machine-specific) and code generation. It is specific to the target hardware, not the source language.
keywords.example: An x86 back-end generates x86 assembly from IR, regardless of whether the IR came from C, Fortran, or Pascal.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="520" height="200" viewBox="0 0 520 200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="20" width="230" height="160" rx="8" class="hi"/><text x="125" y="48" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">FRONT-END</text><text x="125" y="68" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">(Language-specific,</text><text x="125" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">Machine-independent)</text><text x="125" y="108" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Lexical Analysis</text><text x="125" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Syntax Analysis</text><text x="125" y="144" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Semantic Analysis</text><text x="125" y="162" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">IR Generation</text><rect x="280" y="20" width="230" height="160" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="395" y="48" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">BACK-END</text><text x="395" y="68" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">(Language-independent,</text><text x="395" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">Machine-specific)</text><text x="395" y="108" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Code Optimization</text><text x="395" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Code Generation</text><text x="395" y="144" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Register Allocation</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Compiler split into Front-End (language-specific, machine-independent: lexical, syntax, semantic, IR generation) and Back-End (language-independent, machine-specific: optimization, code generation, register allocation).

given.aim: Determine which of S1, S2, S3 correctly describe the front-end and back-end of a compiler

terms.term: S1: Front-end independent of target hardware
terms.meaning: Front-end phases (lexical, syntax, semantic, IR generation) deal with source language and produce machine-independent intermediate representation
terms.example: The same front-end for C can target x86 or ARM by pairing with different back-ends

terms.term: S2: Back-end specific to target hardware
terms.meaning: Back-end phases (instruction selection, register allocation, code generation) depend on the instruction set architecture of the target machine
terms.example: x86 back-end generates different code than ARM back-end from the same IR

terms.term: S3: Back-end specific to programming language
terms.meaning: Back-end operates on intermediate representation (IR), which is language-agnostic. It does NOT depend on the source programming language.
terms.example: An LLVM back-end can compile code from C, C++, Rust, or Swift — all produce LLVM IR
terms.connects: S3 is FALSE — back-end is language-independent, not language-specific

given.plan: Recall the classic compiler architecture. Front-end is language-dependent and machine-independent (S1 TRUE). Back-end is language-independent and machine-dependent (S2 TRUE, S3 FALSE). Answer is B.

steps.title: Check S1: Front-end and target hardware
steps.apply: The front-end performs lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, and intermediate code generation. All these phases deal with the source language and produce IR that is not tied to any target machine. S1 is TRUE.
steps.note: The whole point of IR is to separate language concerns from hardware concerns.

steps.title: Check S2: Back-end and target hardware
steps.apply: The back-end takes IR and produces target machine code. It includes machine-specific optimization (e.g., peephole optimization), instruction selection, register allocation, and code emission — all of which are specific to the target ISA. S2 is TRUE.
steps.note: Different back-ends exist for x86, ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, etc., each producing different assembly.

steps.title: Check S3: Back-end and source programming language
steps.apply: The back-end receives IR, which is language-neutral. It has no knowledge of whether the source was C, Fortran, or Pascal. It is the FRONT-END, not the back-end, that is specific to the programming language. S3 is FALSE.
steps.note: Confusion arises because S3 swaps the property of front-end onto back-end.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: S1 is TRUE, S2 is TRUE, S3 is FALSE. Therefore the answer is B. Only S1 and S2 are TRUE.

solution.result: B. Only S1 and S2 are TRUE.

questions.formula_note: Classic compiler front-end/back-end split: front-end is language-dependent and machine-independent; back-end is language-independent and machine-dependent.

questions.question:
Consider the following two sets:
Set X: P. Lexical Analyzer, Q. Syntax Analyzer, R. Intermediate Code Generator, S. Code Optimizer
Set Y: 1. Abstract Syntax Tree, 2. Token, 3. Parse Tree, 4. Constant Folding

Which one of the following options is the CORRECT match from Set X to Set Y?
A. P -> 4, Q -> 1; R -> 3; S -> 2
B. P -> 2, Q -> 3; R -> 1; S -> 4
C. P -> 2, Q -> 1; R -> 3; S -> 4
D. P -> 4, Q -> 2; R -> 1; S -> 3
questions.to_find: Match each compiler phase in Set X to its associated output/technique in Set Y

understand.plain: Each compiler phase produces a specific output or uses a specific technique. The lexical analyzer produces tokens; the syntax analyzer produces a parse tree; the intermediate code generator produces an abstract syntax tree or IR (here associated with AST); the code optimizer applies techniques such as constant folding. This is a matching question about phase outputs.

keywords.explain: The atomic unit produced by the lexical analyzer from the source code, representing keywords, identifiers, operators, literals, etc.
A tree produced by the syntax analyzer (parser) showing the syntactic structure of the program according to the grammar rules.
keywords.example: For 'a + b * c', the parse tree has '+' at root with 'a' on left and '*' subtree on right.

keywords.explain: A compact tree representation of the program's structure used in semantic analysis and intermediate code generation, omitting unnecessary syntactic details.
keywords.example: AST for 'a + b * c' shows the essential structure without grammar-level detail nodes.

keywords.explain: A code optimization technique where constant expressions are evaluated at compile time rather than runtime.
keywords.example: x = 3 * 4 + 2 is folded to x = 14 by the optimizer.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="540" height="240" viewBox="0 0 540 240"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="220" height="50" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="120" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">P. Lexical Analyzer</text><text x="120" y="50" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">produces</text><rect x="310" y="10" width="220" height="50" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="420" y="38" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">2. Token</text><line x1="230" y1="35" x2="310" y2="35" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/><rect x="10" y="75" width="220" height="50" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="120" y="95" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Q. Syntax Analyzer</text><text x="120" y="115" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">produces</text><rect x="310" y="75" width="220" height="50" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="420" y="103" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">3. Parse Tree</text><line x1="230" y1="100" x2="310" y2="100" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="10" y="140" width="220" height="50" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="120" y="158" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">R. Intermediate Code Gen</text><text x="120" y="178" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">produces</text><rect x="310" y="140" width="220" height="50" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="420" y="168" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">1. Abstract Syntax Tree</text><line x1="230" y1="165" x2="310" y2="165" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="10" y="205" width="220" height="25" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="120" y="222" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">S. Code Optimizer</text><rect x="310" y="205" width="220" height="25" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="420" y="222" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">4. Constant Folding</text><line x1="230" y1="217" x2="310" y2="217" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four matches: Lexical Analyzer -> Token; Syntax Analyzer -> Parse Tree; Intermediate Code Generator -> Abstract Syntax Tree; Code Optimizer -> Constant Folding.

given.aim: Match compiler phases (Set X) to their outputs or associated techniques (Set Y)

terms.meaning: First phase of compilation; scans source text and groups characters into tokens
Checks token sequence against grammar; builds a parse tree showing syntactic derivation
terms.example: Produces a concrete parse tree with all grammar rule nodes

terms.meaning: Translates parse tree or AST into an intermediate representation; often works with or produces an AST
terms.example: Takes parse tree, builds AST or three-address code

terms.meaning: Applies transformations to improve performance; constant folding is a canonical optimization technique

given.plan: Match each phase to what it directly produces or is associated with: Lexical -> Token, Syntax -> Parse Tree, IR Gen -> AST, Optimizer -> Constant Folding. This gives P-2, Q-3, R-1, S-4, which is option B.

steps.title: Match P. Lexical Analyzer
steps.apply: The lexical analyzer (scanner) reads the source program character by character and groups them into meaningful units called tokens (e.g., identifiers, keywords, operators, literals). Output: Token (2). P -> 2.
steps.note: Token is the fundamental output of lexical analysis.

steps.title: Match Q. Syntax Analyzer
steps.apply: The syntax analyzer (parser) takes the token stream and checks it against the grammar rules of the language, building a parse tree (also called concrete syntax tree) that shows the complete syntactic derivation. Output: Parse Tree (3). Q -> 3.
steps.note: Parse tree retains all grammar-level detail; AST is a cleaned-up version used later.

steps.title: Match R. Intermediate Code Generator
steps.apply: The intermediate code generator works with the semantic information and the parse tree to produce an intermediate representation. The Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) is the key data structure at this stage — it strips away unnecessary parse-tree nodes and retains the logical structure for IR generation. Output: Abstract Syntax Tree (1). R -> 1.
steps.note: AST is the bridge between front-end analysis and back-end IR/code generation.

steps.title: Match S. Code Optimizer
steps.apply: The code optimizer applies program transformations to make the code faster or smaller. Constant folding is one of the most fundamental optimization techniques: evaluating constant expressions at compile time. For example, 'x = 2 + 3' becomes 'x = 5'. Technique: Constant Folding (4). S -> 4.
steps.note: Other optimizations include dead code elimination, common subexpression elimination, loop invariant code motion.

steps.title: Final matching
steps.apply: P -> 2 (Token), Q -> 3 (Parse Tree), R -> 1 (Abstract Syntax Tree), S -> 4 (Constant Folding). This matches option B.

questions.formula_note: Phase-output associations: Lexical Analyzer produces Tokens; Syntax Analyzer produces Parse Tree; IR Generator uses Abstract Syntax Tree; Code Optimizer applies Constant Folding.
